text: The adrenal gland is a small gland that sits just above the kidney in the back of the abdomen. Just like you have two kidneys, you have two adrenal glands. The purpose of the adrenal gland is to secrete hormones that affect the way your body manages blood pressure, salt and kidney function, as well as stress.

Occasionally, people can develop tumors of the adrenal gland. Although these tumors are usually benign, they can sometimes cause over-secretion of one or more of the hormones produced by the adrenal gland. When this happens, the treatment for this is to remove the adrenal gland that has the tumor. This is called an adrenalectomy.

This video is of a real adrenalectomy performed at TriHealth. It is being performed minimally invasively using robotic technology. This video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical adrenalectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how the surgery is actually performed. If you are scheduled to have this procedure, your surgery will be performed in a similar fashion.

The robotic instruments are positioned, revealing the splenic flexure of the colon near the spleen. The transverse colon is visible with a layer of omentum fat hanging off it. The surgeon begins to expose the adrenal gland, a retroperitoneal organ located at the back of the abdomen, by moving surrounding structures out of the way.

The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery instrument to mobilize the spleen and distal pancreas, moving them medially to the left of the screen. The spleen is held up with the left arm, and the electrocautery allows for hooking behind the tissue, cauterizing, and dividing it to expose the adrenal gland.

The surgeon separates the layers of fat between the inferior border of the pancreas and the colon, visible on the right side of the screen, to expose the adrenal gland. The adrenal gland is partially visible, appearing as a yellowish-orange organ, slightly different in color from the surrounding abdominal fat.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal gland above the kidney, visible as a bulge to the right of the screen. Blood vessels, including the renal vein, run horizontally across the field. The adrenal tumor is exposed, appearing larger than normal due to its benign nature. The pancreas and spleen are lifted to access the adrenal gland, which is tucked behind these organs.
The surgeon highlights the orange color of the adrenal gland, contrasting it with the yellow fat surrounding tissue and the yellow hue of the pancreas.

The surgeon has fully exposed the adrenal tumor, with the spleen and distal pancreas lifted away. The renal vein, a large vein running left to right, and the smaller adrenal vein, running north to south, are visible. The surgeon is exposing the superior border of the renal vein to prepare for dividing the adrenal vein while keeping the renal vein intact. The adrenal gland extends down to the renal vein, and the tissue above it will be removed.

The surgeon clears surrounding tissue to place clips on the adrenal vein for ligation and division. After dividing the vein, the surgeon separates the surrounding tissue from the adrenal gland, aiming to remove the surrounding fat along with the gland. The inferior pole of the adrenal gland is held up with the surgeon's left hand.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer, an energy device that cauterizes and divides tissue, to remove the surrounding fat and tissue with the adrenal gland. The kidney is visible at the bottom right of the screen, appearing as a reddish-brown organ.
The surgeon removes the surrounding fat from the diaphragm and retroperitoneal muscles, ensuring complete clearance of tissue around the adrenal tumor.
The surgeon prepares to divide the remaining tissue to free the adrenal gland. A surgical pouch is introduced through a trocar to encase the adrenal gland for removal from the abdominal cavity.
The spleen and pancreas are repositioned back into their anatomical locations following the completion of the robotic left adrenalectomy.
